answer:
In Chapter 7 of *Traité de la propriété* Vol. I, Charles Comte designates a nation's territory as the highest form of property—after things common to all humanity—due to its vast extent and critical importance, as it encompasses and protects all subordinate properties (provincial, communal, familial, and individual).[^1] He observes that, since remote antiquity, every habitable part of the globe has been occupied by nations or tribes (civilized or barbarous), formed from families grouping into hordes, each claiming exclusive property over a delimited territory essential to its survival.[^2]

Comte emphasizes that this national property is universally recognized and nearly indestructible: even egalitarians demanding equal shares within a nation do not contest territories between nations, viewing such claims as tantamount to extermination; civilized nations' territories, developed through population growth and prosperity, are especially incontestable.[^3] While violence and conquest have violated territories throughout history (e.g., Roman expansions), such acts do not negate the natural fact of occupation, and treaties consecrating them lack moral force unless aligned with natural justice—nations owe primary duties to their own members.[^4]

The guarantee of a nation's territory lies not in governments or civil laws, but in natural necessities: self-defense, geographic barriers (seas, mountains), allied interests, and insurmountable obstacles to invasion, collectively forming the "law of nations."[^5]

[^1]: Charles Comte, *Traité de la propriété*, [paragraph](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-la-propriete-vol-i-2c7a99/07-du-territoire-propre-a-chaque-nation/translation/#p-2cfb18).

[^2]: Charles Comte, *Traité de la propriété*, [paragraph](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-la-propriete-vol-i-2c7a99/07-du-territoire-propre-a-chaque-nation/translation/#p-b9fe30).

[^3]: Charles Comte, *Traité de la propriété*, [paragraph](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-la-propriete-vol-i-2c7a99/07-du-territoire-propre-a-chaque-nation/translation/#p-0a6e26).

[^4]: Charles Comte, *Traité de la propriété*, [paragraph](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-la-propriete-vol-i-2c7a99/07-du-territoire-propre-a-chaque-nation/translation/#p-dc0e30).

[^5]: Charles Comte, *Traité de la propriété*, [paragraph](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-la-propriete-vol-i-2c7a99/07-du-territoire-propre-a-chaque-nation/translation/#p-982e4a).
